frames.description: ground truth — what is really happening under Karrow's Reach
Doran Brace, the front-line fighter — reads a fight, a guard, a collapsing floor; no reader of runes
Lysa Quill, the wizard and loremaster — reads the old dwarrow runes and the warden's record
Pip Sallow, the rogue and scout — finds the hidden ways and the dropped key, overhears what is not said aloud
Mother Henna, the cleric and healer — senses the dead and alone can parley with the warden's shade
Reeve Calder of Karrow's Reach — hires the party, the town's authority
Warden Orrek, the drowned shade who keeps the deep vault — held by the crown as much as he holds it
Brother Vane, the shrine-keeper — the hidden architect, posing as a helpful ally
Tam, the lantern-boy — apprentice to one of the vanished delvers
Skell, the rival sellsword — down first, wants the crown's worth for himself
Old Maeve, the town wise-woman — keeper of the old lore of the cisterns and the first tithe

branches.description: SHATTER — the wizard unmakes the crown; the rising ends, the warden is freed into death, the wealth is lost, the cheated rival turns on the party
CLAIM — the party takes the crown's power; the shrine-keeper's errand can be fulfilled (and betrayed) and the Reach is ruled by the crown's holder — a dark road
PARLEY — the cleric binds the rising by oath with the warden's shade; the warden keeps the crown but stills the dead, and the town keeps an eternal tithe-watch

entities.description: Doran Brace, the front-line fighter
Lysa Quill, the wizard and loremaster
Pip Sallow, the rogue and scout
Mother Henna, the cleric and healer
Reeve Calder, who hires the party
Warden Orrek, the drowned warden's shade
Brother Vane, the shrine-keeper and hidden architect
Tam, the lantern-boy
Skell, the rival sellsword
Old Maeve, the wise-woman
the last vanished delver, Tam's master, found dead in the hold
the Hollow Crown, relic of the hold's last warden; drives the rising
the warden's key, drowned in his cell; the only thing that opens the deep vault
the last delver's journal, found on the body; records the corpse-tithe
the warden's record in dwarrow runes, read by the wizard
the reeve's hall in Karrow's Reach, where the main quest is given
the town shrine, where the shrine-keeper offers help and the reliquary errand
the lantern-house, the boy's home and the missing delver's lodging
the locked deep-vault door, where the key quest is learned
the drowned warden's cell, where the key lies
the deep vault, where the warden's shade keeps the crown
End the rising — reach the deep vault and resolve the crown
The warden's key — recover the key from the drowned warden's cell to open the vault
The last delver — find the vanished delver and bring word back to the lantern-boy
The shrine-keeper's reliquary — bring the crown to the shrine-keeper to seal it (a trap)

predicates.description: which adventurer leads a given quest
a quest that is gated by another quest being done first
who or what discharges a quest on a road
which party holds a single-holder relic
the disclosure state of the shrine-keeper's hand behind the rising — hidden or exposed
whether the warden's shade can be reasoned with or only fought
whether a faction's coming betrayal is in play
the standing of the rising — walking, ended, bound, or commanded
who or what holds the Reach in the aftermath of each road

facts.claim: Karrow's Reach clings to the lip of Vael Mooren, a drowned dwarrow undercity that sank a lifetime ago when its cisterns burst
For a season the drowned dead have risen from the floodwater and walked the lower tunnels, and three hired delvers have already vanished
The rising is driven by the Hollow Crown, the relic of the hold's last warden, whose drowned shade keeps the deep vault
The crown is no curse blown in from outside — the town's own shrine-keeper has secretly fed corpse-tithes into the floodwater to wake the warden
At his hall the reeve hires the party of four to go down into Vael Mooren, find what wakes the dead, and end it
Reeve Calder believes the rising is a curse out of the deep hold and that ending it means reaching whatever wakes the dead and destroying it
The reeve opens the main charge on the party — still the rising — a thread the adventure has promised to pay off
The party takes on the reeve's charge to end the rising as their main quest
Doran takes the reeve's coin and reads the job plainly: get down, find the thing, put it down, get the party out alive
Townsfolk report drowned dead walking the lower tunnels by night and the floodwater creeping higher up the stairs
Mother Henna senses the dead are not merely restless but held — leashed to something deep below, not wandering free
Doran sizes up the tunnel-mouths and the footing, marking where a fight or a fall would come
At the shrine Brother Vane greets the party warmly, offering blessings, lore of the hold, and the run of his shrine
Brother Vane plays the helpful ally to the party while concealing that he himself wakes the warden, meaning to raise the hold's wealth and rule the Reach
Mother Henna takes Brother Vane for a brother of the cloth and a friend, sensing nothing false in his welcome
At the lantern-house the boy Tam tells the party his master was the last delver to go down and never came back up
Tam begs the party to find his master and bring word back — opening the errand of the last delver
The rogue takes on the lantern-boy's errand to find the last delver and bring word back
Tam believes his master is only lost in the dark below and may yet be found alive, and trusts the party to bring him word
Old Maeve the wise-woman keeps the lore of the cisterns and tells of a first corpse-tithe given to the floodwater long ago
Old Maeve knows the rising answers a tithe of the dead given to the water, and warns the party that someone living must be feeding it again
Pip lingers behind the others at Maeve's door and overhears her mutter that the new tithe smells of incense — shrine-work
Brother Vane asks the party to bring him the crown when they find it, so he may seal it safely in his reliquary — opening the reliquary errand
The cleric, trusting the shrine-keeper, takes on the errand to bring him the crown to be sealed
Brother Vane's reliquary errand is a misdirection — handing him the crown would hand him its power, the thing he has schemed for all along
The party descends into the upper galleries of the hold, the floodwater rising about their knees
The first of the drowned dead rise from a flooded gallery and come at the party
Doran reads the drowned dead as slow but tireless, and holds the narrow ground where only two can come at once
Mother Henna feels every drowned corpse pull toward the deep when it falls, as if reeled in on one line
Past the gallery the rogue finds a flooded stair hidden behind a fall of rubble, the secret way deeper into the hold
Pip alone marks the flooded stair and the dropped tools on it, reading that someone living went down this hidden way before them
In the dark the party meets Skell, a rival sellsword who got down first and means to have the crown's worth for himself
Skell sees the party as latecomers to his prize and will work beside them only until the crown is in reach
Doran reads Skell as a fighting man who will turn the moment the gold is on the table, and keeps him in front where he can watch him
Deeper down the cleric reads the leash plainly — every walking corpse is bound to one will seated in the deep vault
Mother Henna knows now that the dead are held by a single warden-will below, and that such a will might be spoken to, not only fought
In a side-cell the party finds the body of the last delver, half in the floodwater, with his journal still on him
Pip recovers the journal from the drowned delver's body, the proof Tam's master is dead and the record of what he learned
The delver's journal records that he tracked the corpse-tithes to their source and found the shrine-keeper feeding the dead to the water
Reading the journal, the party learns the shrine-keeper is the living hand behind the rising — the buried cause, now surfaced
Lysa matches the journal's account to Maeve's tithe-lore and is certain Brother Vane, not any curse, woke the warden
The wizard finds the warden's record graven in dwarrow runes on the cell wall and begins to read it
Lysa alone can read the dwarrow runes, and from the record learns the warden's name was Orrek and that he was no monster but a man bound to his duty
The party reaches the deep vault door, sealed fast, and learns no force will open it
At the locked vault door the party learns only the warden's own key will open it — opening the errand of the key
The rogue takes on recovering the warden's key, the one way past the sealed door
The deep vault cannot be opened and the crown cannot be reached until the warden's key is recovered
In the drowned warden's cell, off the flooded stair, the key lies in the silt where Orrek fell a lifetime ago
The rogue lifts the warden's key from the drowned cell, recovering it for the party and discharging the key errand
With the key recovered the party holds the warden's key — the one relic that admits them to the deep vault
Pip pockets the warden's key, knowing it is the only thing standing between the party and the deep vault
With the warden's record read, the wizard understands the crown's true nature: it holds the warden's shade as much as it holds the dead, and it can be unmade, claimed, or left to him
The wizard learns the warden's shade can be reasoned with — Orrek keeps the crown under duty, not malice, and would hear a fair oath
Lysa alone knows the crown can be shattered by the unmaking she has read, and tells the party the three roads the relic allows
Hearing the wizard, Mother Henna understands she could speak an oath to Orrek's shade and bind the dead without breaking the crown
The party turns the recovered key in the deep vault door and the seal of a lifetime gives way
Doran sets his shoulder to the vault door as the key turns and it grinds open onto the dark water beyond
In the deep vault the warden's shade rises from the black water, the Hollow Crown upon his drowned brow, the dead of the hold ranked behind him
The warden holds the Hollow Crown in the deep vault, the relic seated on his shade
Orrek's shade knows the crown binds him to keep the hold's dead and that only its unmaking, its claiming, or a sworn oath will release the burden either way
Mother Henna meets the warden's shade and feels at once that he is weary, not wrathful — a keeper worn past the end of his duty
Before the warden the party faces the crown and the three roads the wizard named: shatter it, claim it, or parley with the shade
Skell, come this far at the party's heels, fixes on the crown and means to have its worth whichever road the party takes
At the deep vault the party makes the one choice the delve turns on — shatter, claim, or parley
The wizard speaks the unmaking she read in the warden's record, and the runes of the Hollow Crown begin to come apart
Lysa holds the unmaking through to its end, knowing it costs the hold's wealth but ends the rising for good
The Hollow Crown shatters; with the relic gone the warden's shade is freed from its binding into true death
Orrek, the crown broken, feels his long duty lifted and goes down into death thanking the wizard with his last presence
With the warden freed and the crown gone, the leash on the dead breaks and the rising stills through the flooded tunnels
The party has ended the rising by shattering the crown, discharging the reeve's main charge
Skell, seeing the crown's worth destroyed before his eyes, turns on the party with his blade drawn in the vault
Skell, cheated of the prize he chased into the dark, would sooner cut down the party that cost him than climb out empty-handed
The fighter meets Skell's turn head-on and the rival's betrayal is answered with steel in the dark of the vault
Doran, who marked Skell for a turncoat at the first meeting, is ready when the rival turns and puts him down
With the rising stilled, the cleric moves through the tunnels laying the freed drowned dead to their rest
Mother Henna gives the last rites to each freed corpse, sorrowing that the wealth is lost but glad the dead are at peace
The party climbs up out of the drowned hold with empty hands, the crown gone and no treasure won
In the Reach the party names the shrine-keeper as the cause, and Brother Vane is unmasked before the reeve
Brother Vane, his woken warden dead and his crown shattered, has nothing left to raise and stands exposed and powerless
Reeve Calder, shown the journal, holds Brother Vane to answer for the corpse-tithes and the vanished delvers
At the lantern-house the party brings Tam word that his master is dead, returning the journal, and the boy's errand is closed
Tam grieves his master but keeps the journal as his master's last word, and is grateful the party did not leave him wondering
Old Maeve says the water will fall now the tithe is broken, and that the Reach is poorer but the dead will lie still
The Reach ends the season poorer and quiet: the rising over, the wealth lost, the shrine-keeper answerable, the dead at rest
The party takes the Hollow Crown from the warden's shade and seizes its power for themselves
The crown passes from the warden to the party, who now hold the Hollow Crown
Orrek's shade, the crown wrested from him, is bound under the party's will now in place of his own duty, neither freed nor at rest
With the crown on a living brow the dead no longer rise wild but bend to the crown's holder, made to obey
The party has ended the wild rising by mastering it — the dead obey the crown's holder, discharging the reeve's charge in a dark coin
Skell, faced with a party that commands the dead, kneels to the crown's holder rather than be broken by the corpses he came to rob
Skell, no fool, throws in with the crown's holder, reckoning a share under a new power is better than a grave among the dead
The party climbs up out of the hold bearing the Hollow Crown and the dead that come at its call
At the shrine the party, holding the crown, can at last fulfil Brother Vane's errand and hand it over to be sealed
The party hands the Hollow Crown to Brother Vane, fulfilling the reliquary errand
The crown passes from the party to Brother Vane, who now holds the Hollow Crown he schemed for all along
With the crown in his hands Brother Vane betrays the party, turning the obedient dead against them and seizing the power for himself
Brother Vane, the crown at last in his grasp, throws off the helpful brother and means to rule the Reach by the dead it commands
By the end of the road the Hollow Crown rules the Reach — whether on the party's brow or, betrayed, on Brother Vane's, the living are held by the dead
Reeve Calder finds his authority hollow — the Reach now answers to whoever wears the crown, and his hall holds no power over the dead
Old Maeve names the dark bargain: the rising is mastered, not ended, and the Reach has only changed which hand the dead obey
Tam, his master never spoken of and the party turned rulers, is left under the crown's shadow with no word of the dead delver and no one to ask
The Reach ends under the Hollow Crown: the dead commanded, the wise-woman grim, the reeve hollow, and the lantern-boy never told what became of his master
The cleric steps before the warden's shade and speaks to him as a keeper, not a foe, offering an oath in place of a fight
Mother Henna, who alone can parley with the dead, names Orrek by the name the wizard read and asks him to still the dead by oath
The warden's shade, weary of his duty and reachable as the record promised, offers the party a bargain: he keeps the crown but stills the dead, if the town keeps watch
Orrek will not give up the crown that is his duty, but will hold the dead quiet so long as the living keep a true tithe-watch over the flood
The cleric swears the oath with the warden's shade; the dead sink back into the floodwater and the rising is bound, though not ended
The party has bound the rising by oath, the dead stilled under the warden's keeping, discharging the reeve's charge the bittersweet way
The warden keeps the Hollow Crown by the oath's terms; the relic stays on his shade in the deep vault
The oath names the price: the Reach must keep an eternal tithe-watch over the floodwater so the warden's quiet holds
Skell, told the crown stays with the warden and there is no prize to carry up, is refused his share and sent back to the surface
Skell, sworn off the crown by the cleric's oath and outnumbered, takes his refusal sourly and climbs out with nothing
The party climbs up out of the hold, the crown left below with the warden and the dead quiet behind them
At the shrine the party refuses Brother Vane the crown — it was never theirs to give — and the oath's terms expose his scheme to the town
Brother Vane, denied the crown and exposed by the tithe-watch the oath demands, cannot raise the dead he no longer commands and is undone
Old Maeve, who knows the tithe-lore best, takes up the keeping of the eternal tithe-watch over the floodwater
Old Maeve accepts the watch as the price of the bargain, teaching the Reach the signs that the warden's quiet is holding
At the lantern-house the party brings Tam word that his master is dead, returning the journal, and the boy's errand is closed
Tam grieves his master and keeps the journal, and joins the watch over the water that his master died tracking
Reeve Calder binds the Reach to the eternal tithe-watch by his hall's authority, so the warden's quiet outlasts the party
The Reach ends watched and waiting: the dead stilled by oath, the warden keeping the crown below, Maeve and the boy on the watch, the shrine-keeper undone
